full_context: The design safety factor of conductors and ground wires at the lowest point of sag shall not be less than 2.5, and the design safety factor at the suspension point shall not be less than 2.25. The design safety factor of the ground wire shall not be less than that of the conductor. The maximum tension of conductors and ground wires at the lowest point of sag shall be calculated as: T_max ≤ T_p/K_c. Where: T_max is the maximum tension at the lowest point of sag (N); T_p is the breaking force of the conductor/ground wire (N); K_c is the design safety factor of the conductor/ground wire.

variable_definitions.name: Maximum tension of conductor/ground wire
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum tension of the conductor/ground wire at the lowest point of sag

variable_definitions.name: Breaking force
variable_definitions.meaning: Ultimate tensile strength (breaking load) of the conductor/ground wire

variable_definitions.name: Design safety factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Safety margin factor for conductor/ground wire, not less than 2.5

potential_questions: Given conductor breaking force T_p=85kN and safety factor K_c=2.5, find maximum allowable tension T_max
task_description: 1. Determine conductor breaking force \(T_p\). 2. Determine design safety factor \(K_c\). 3. Compute \(T_{\max}=T_p/K_c\). 4. Output the maximum allowable tension value \(T_{\max}\).

full_context: For insulation coordination design of compact lines, the creepage-distance-per-kilovolt method or the pollution withstand voltage method may be used to select an appropriate insulator type and number of units. When using the creepage-distance-per-kilovolt method, the number of insulator units shall be calculated as: n ≥ (λU)/(K_e L_0). Where: n is the required number of units per insulator string at an altitude of 1000m; λ is the creepage distance per kilovolt (cm/kV); U is the nominal system voltage (kV); K_e is the effective coefficient of insulator creepage distance; L_0 is the geometric creepage distance of a single suspension insulator unit (cm).

variable_definitions.name: Number of insulator units
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum required number of units per insulator string

variable_definitions.name: Creepage distance per kilovolt
variable_definitions.meaning: Required creepage distance per unit voltage

variable_definitions.name: Nominal system voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated voltage of the power system

variable_definitions.name: Effective coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Utilization efficiency of insulator creepage distance

variable_definitions.name: Geometric creepage distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Surface creepage distance of a single insulator unit

potential_questions: In a Class III pollution area (λ=2.5cm/kV), for a 500kV substation (K_e=0.9, L_0=45cm), find required number of insulator units
task_description: 1. Determine \(\lambda\) corresponding to the pollution level. 2. Obtain system voltage \(U\). 3. Determine parameters \(K_e\) and \(L_0\). 4. Compute \(n=\lceil \lambda U/(K_e L_0) \rceil\). 5. Output the minimum number of units \(n\).

full_context: The altitude correction factor K_a for air discharge voltage can be calculated as: K_a = e^(mH/8150). Where: H is altitude (m); m is the altitude correction factor. For power-frequency and lightning impulse voltages, m=1.0. For switching overvoltage, the altitude correction factor m can be selected from curves a and c in the graph relating m to voltage.

variable_definitions.name: Altitude correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor for air discharge voltage in high-altitude areas

variable_definitions.name: Altitude
variable_definitions.meaning: Elevation of the site location

variable_definitions.name: Correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction coefficient related to voltage type

potential_questions: At altitude 3000m, switching overvoltage correction factor m=0.85; find correction factor K_a
task_description: 1. Determine altitude \(H\). 2. Select \(m\) based on voltage type. 3. Compute exponent term \(mH/8150\). 4. Compute \(K_a=e^{mH/8150}\). 5. Output the correction factor \(K_a\).

full_context: Anti-buoyancy stability calculation for underground substations: 0.9(G_sk + G_ek) ≥ 1.05 P_buo A. Where: G_sk is the characteristic value of the self-weight of the structure/building (kN); G_ek is the characteristic value of the self-weight of overburden/backfill soil (kN); A is the base slab area of the structural calculation unit (m²); P_buo is the buoyant uplift pressure of groundwater acting on the base slab area of the structural calculation unit (kN/m²).

variable_definitions.name: Structural self-weight
variable_definitions.meaning: Characteristic value of the building/structure self-weight

variable_definitions.name: Overburden soil self-weight
variable_definitions.meaning: Characteristic value of the weight of backfilled soil

variable_definitions.name: Buoyant uplift pressure
variable_definitions.meaning: Uplift pressure produced by groundwater

variable_definitions.name: Base slab area
variable_definitions.meaning: Base area of the building foundation slab

potential_questions: An underground station has G_sk=15000kN, G_ek=8000kN, A=1200m², P_buo=15kN/m²; check anti-buoyancy stability
task_description: 1. Determine structural self-weight \(G_{sk}\). 2. Determine overburden soil weight \(G_{ek}\). 3. Measure base slab area \(A\). 4. Determine groundwater uplift pressure \(P_{\text{buo}}\). 5. Compute left side \(0.9(G_{sk}+G_{ek})\). 6. Compute right side \(1.05\,P_{\text{buo}}\,A\). 7. Compare the two sides.

full_context: The design safety factor of conductors and ground wires at the lowest point of sag shall not be less than 2.5. The maximum tension of conductors and ground wires at the lowest point of sag shall be calculated as: T_max ≤ T_p/K_c. Where: T_max is the maximum tension at the lowest point of sag (N); T_p is the breaking force of the conductor/ground wire (N); K_c is the design safety factor of the conductor/ground wire.

variable_definitions.name: Maximum tension
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum allowable operating tension of conductor/ground wire

variable_definitions.name: Breaking force
variable_definitions.meaning: Ultimate breaking force of conductor/ground wire

variable_definitions.name: Safety factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Design safety margin factor

potential_questions: For ACSR with T_p=120kN and safety factor K_c=2.8, find maximum allowable tension
task_description: 1. Obtain conductor breaking force \(T_p\). 2. Determine safety factor \(K_c\). 3. Compute \(T_{\max}=T_p/K_c\). 4. Output allowable tension value \(T_{\max}\).

full_context: Substation insulation configuration is based on the pollution zone distribution map, and the number of insulator units shall be calculated as: n ≥ (λU)/(K_e L_0). Where: n is the required number of units per insulator string at an altitude of 1000m; λ is the creepage distance per kilovolt (cm/kV); U is the nominal system voltage (kV); K_e is the effective coefficient of insulator creepage distance; L_0 is the geometric creepage distance of a single suspension insulator unit (cm).

variable_definitions.name: Number of insulator units
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum required number of units per insulator string

variable_definitions.name: Creepage distance per kilovolt
variable_definitions.meaning: Required creepage distance per unit voltage

variable_definitions.name: System voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Nominal voltage of the power system

variable_definitions.name: Effective coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Creepage distance utilization efficiency

variable_definitions.name: Creepage distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Geometric creepage distance of a single insulator unit

potential_questions: A 220kV substation is in pollution class d (λ=3.1cm/kV). For XP-70 insulator L_0=29cm and K_e=1.0, find required number of units
task_description: 1. Determine pollution class and \(\lambda\). 2. Obtain system voltage \(U\). 3. Determine parameters \(K_e\) and \(L_0\). 4. Compute \(n=\lceil \lambda U/(K_e L_0) \rceil\). 5. Output the minimum number of units \(n\).

full_context: When substation electrical equipment is installed at an altitude above 1000m (but not exceeding 4000m) and the maximum ambient air temperature is 40°C, the allowable temperature rise shall be reduced by 0.3% for each additional 100m above 1000m. The altitude correction factor K is calculated as: K = 1/(1.1 - H/10000). This formula is used to correct the effect of high altitude on temperature rise, ensuring safe operation in plateau environments.

variable_definitions.name: Altitude correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Temperature-rise correction factor for high-altitude areas

variable_definitions.name: Altitude
variable_definitions.meaning: Elevation of the equipment installation site

potential_questions: A substation is at altitude 2500m; find the temperature-rise correction factor K
task_description: 1. Determine altitude \(H=2500\,\mathrm{m}\). 2. Compute denominator \(1.1-H/10000=1.1-0.25=0.85\). 3. Compute \(K=1/0.85\approx 1.176\). 4. Output correction factor \(K\).

full_context: Thermal stability verification of bare conductors shall satisfy: S ≥ √Q_d/C. Where Q_d is the thermal effect of short-circuit current (A²·s) and C is the thermal stability coefficient. This formula ensures that the conductor can withstand the thermal effect during a short-circuit fault without damage, and is applicable to design verification of various bare conductors in substations.

variable_definitions.name: Conductor cross-sectional area
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum allowable cross-sectional area of the conductor

variable_definitions.name: Thermal effect of short-circuit current
variable_definitions.meaning: Integral value of thermal effect produced by short-circuit current

variable_definitions.name: Thermal stability coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Thermal stability parameter related to material characteristics

potential_questions: Given Q_d=2.5×10⁶ A²·s and aluminum conductor C=87, find minimum conductor cross-sectional area
task_description: 1. Determine \(Q_d\). 2. Determine material coefficient \(C\) (take 87 for aluminum). 3. Compute \(\sqrt{Q_d}\approx 1581\). 4. Compute \(S_{\min}=1581/87\approx 18.17\,\mathrm{mm}^2\). 5. Select a standard cross-section \(S\ge S_{\min}\).

full_context: Current density limits for contact surfaces of unplated joints: when the operating current is less than 200A, the current density for copper-to-copper contact surfaces is 0.31A/mm²; the current density for aluminum-to-aluminum contact surfaces is calculated by: J_Al = 0.78J_Cu. This ensures joints do not overheat during long-term operation and is applicable to conductor connection design in substations.

variable_definitions.name: Aluminum contact surface current density
variable_definitions.meaning: Allowable current density on aluminum conductor contact surface

variable_definitions.name: Copper contact surface current density
variable_definitions.meaning: Allowable current density on copper conductor contact surface

potential_questions: Given copper contact surface J_Cu=0.31A/mm², find allowable aluminum contact surface current density
task_description: 1. Determine copper conductor current density \(J_{Cu}\). 2. Compute \(J_{Al}=0.78\times J_{Cu}=0.78\times 0.31\approx 0.242\,\mathrm{A/mm}^2\). 3. Output allowable current density \(J_{Al}\).

full_context: Dynamic stability verification of rigid conductors shall satisfy that the maximum stress does not exceed the allowable value: σ_max ≤ [σ]. Allowable stress values for different materials: hard-drawn copper 120MPa; aluminum alloy 1035-H112 35MPa. This ensures the mechanical strength of switchgear structures under short-circuit electromagnetic forces.

variable_definitions.name: Maximum calculated stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum stress produced by short-circuit electromagnetic forces

variable_definitions.name: Allowable stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Ultimate stress value the material is allowed to withstand

potential_questions: An aluminum alloy support has σ_max=28MPa under short-circuit electromagnetic force; check whether it meets the requirement ([σ]=35MPa)
task_description: 1. Determine material allowable stress \([\sigma]\). 2. Calculate actual stress \(\sigma_{\max}\). 3. Check \(\sigma_{\max}\le [\sigma]\). 4. Output verification result.

full_context: Breeze-induced vibration check formula for outdoor tubular conductors: v_js = fD/A. Where f is the natural frequency (Hz), D is the outer diameter of the tube (m), and A is the frequency coefficient (take 0.214 for a circular tube). If the calculated wind speed is less than 6m/s, anti-vibration measures shall be taken.

variable_definitions.name: Resonance calculation wind speed
variable_definitions.meaning: Critical wind speed that triggers breeze resonance

variable_definitions.name: Natural frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: First-order vibration frequency of the conductor

variable_definitions.name: Tube outer diameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Outer diameter of the tubular conductor

variable_definitions.name: Frequency coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient related to vibration mode

potential_questions: Given tube diameter D=0.2m and f=2.5Hz, find critical wind speed v_js
task_description: 1. Determine \(f\). 2. Determine \(D\). 3. Take \(A=0.214\). 4. Compute \(v_{js}=fD/A=2.5\times 0.2/0.214\approx 2.34\,\mathrm{m/s}\). 5. Output critical wind speed \(v_{js}\).

full_context: Formula for selecting the small neutral reactor reactance of a shunt reactor: X_0 = X_L²/(X_12 - 3X_L). It is used to accelerate extinction of the secondary arc, where X_L is the positive-sequence reactance of the shunt reactor and X_12 is the line phase-to-phase capacitive reactance. This formula is applicable to single-phase reclosing system design for 330kV and above lines.

variable_definitions.name: Small reactance value
variable_definitions.meaning: Impedance value of the small neutral reactor reactance

variable_definitions.name: Shunt reactor reactance
variable_definitions.meaning: Positive-sequence reactance of the line shunt reactor

variable_definitions.name: Phase-to-phase capacitive reactance
variable_definitions.meaning: Capacitive reactance corresponding to the line phase-to-phase capacitance

potential_questions: Given X_L=1500Ω and X_12=2000Ω, find the optimal small reactance value
task_description: 1. Determine \(X_L\). 2. Determine \(X_{12}\). 3. Compute denominator \(X_{12}-3X_L\). 4. Compute numerator \(X_L^2\). 5. Compute \(X_0=X_L^2/(X_{12}-3X_L)\). 6. Output reactance value (take absolute value if needed).

full_context: Under the maximum transient current, the maximum step voltage at any point on the surface of the grounding electrode site shall satisfy Eq. (4.3.3): E_my = 5 + 0.03ρ_s. Where E_my is the maximum allowable step voltage on the ground surface (V/m), and ρ_s is the surface-layer soil resistivity (Ω·m). If the maximum step voltage at any ground-surface point does not meet the requirement, isolation measures shall be taken.

variable_definitions.name: Maximum allowable step voltage on ground surface
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum safe step-voltage limit tolerable by the human body

variable_definitions.name: Surface-layer soil resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Electrical resistivity characteristic of the surface soil layer

potential_questions: Given soil resistivity ρ_s=100Ω·m, calculate maximum allowable step voltage E_my
task_description: 1. Obtain surface-layer soil resistivity \(\rho_s\). 2. Compute \(0.03\times\rho_s\). 3. Compute \(E_{\mathrm{my}}=5+0.03\rho_s\). 4. Output \(E_{\mathrm{my}}\).

full_context: The minimum burial depth of a grounding electrode should generally be determined based on controlling ground-surface step voltage. When the electrode length is much greater than the electrode burial depth, the minimum burial depth can be approximately calculated by Eq. (7.1.8): h = (ρ_s τ)/(2π E_my). Where h is the minimum burial depth (m); ρ_s is the equivalent soil resistivity of the ground surface (Ω·m); τ is the grounding electrode leakage current density (A/m); E_my is the maximum allowable ground-surface step voltage (V/m).

variable_definitions.name: Burial depth
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum burial depth of the grounding electrode

variable_definitions.name: Equivalent soil resistivity at ground surface
variable_definitions.meaning: Comprehensive resistivity characteristic of the surface soil layer

variable_definitions.name: Leakage current density
variable_definitions.meaning: Current per unit length of the feeding element leaking into the earth

variable_definitions.name: Maximum allowable step voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Human-safe step voltage limit

potential_questions: Given ρ_s=80Ω·m, τ=2A/m, E_my=8V/m, calculate minimum burial depth h
task_description: 1. Obtain \(\rho_s\), \(\tau\), and \(E_{\mathrm{my}}\). 2. Compute numerator \(\rho_s\times\tau\). 3. Compute denominator \(2\pi E_{\mathrm{my}}\). 4. Compute \(h=(\rho_s\times\tau)/(2\pi E_{\mathrm{my}})\). 5. Output \(h\).

full_context: For grounding electrodes operating for a long time with earth return, temperature rise should be controlled by grounding resistance, and grounding resistance shall satisfy Eq. (7.2.4): R_e ≤ R_0 = (1/I_N)√[2λ_m (ρ_d²/ρ_m)(θ_my - θ_c)]. Where: R_e is the grounding resistance of the electrode to remote earth (Ω); R_0 is the critical grounding resistance (Ω); I_N is the normal rated current (A); λ_m is the soil thermal conductivity of the electrode burial layer (W/(m·°C)); ρ_d is the equivalent soil resistivity at the electrode site (Ω·m); ρ_m is the soil resistivity of the electrode burial layer (Ω·m); θ_my is the maximum allowable temperature of the grounding electrode (°C); θ_c is the maximum natural soil temperature (°C).

variable_definitions.name: Critical grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Grounding resistance threshold ensuring safe temperature rise

variable_definitions.name: Normal rated current
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated operating current of the system

variable_definitions.name: Soil thermal conductivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Heat conduction capability of the soil

variable_definitions.name: Overall equivalent resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Comprehensive resistivity characteristic of electrode-site soil

variable_definitions.name: Burial-layer resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Soil resistivity at the electrode burial depth/layer

variable_definitions.name: Maximum allowable temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum allowable operating temperature of the grounding electrode material

variable_definitions.name: Maximum natural soil temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum natural temperature of the surrounding soil

potential_questions: Given I_N=3000A, λ_m=1.5W/(m·°C), ρ_d=50Ω·m, ρ_m=60Ω·m, θ_my=90°C, θ_c=30°C, calculate critical grounding resistance R_0
task_description: 1. Compute temperature difference \(\Delta\theta=\theta_{\mathrm{my}}-\theta_c\). 2. Compute \(\rho_d^2/\rho_m\). 3. Compute \(2\lambda_m\times(\rho_d^2/\rho_m)\times\Delta\theta\). 4. Take square root of the result from step 3. 5. Compute \(R_0=(1/I_N)\times\) (result of step 4). 6. Output \(R_0\).

full_context: For grounding electrodes that operate for a short time with earth return, the side length of the coke (charcoal) cross section at point p shall satisfy Eq. (7.2.5): S_p ≥ k ρ_m τ_p √[T_0/(16 ρ_p C_p (θ_my − θ_c))]. Where: S_p is the side length of the coke cross section at point p (m); k is the coordination factor (see Appendix A.2); τ_p is the leakage current density at point p (A/m); ρ_p is the soil resistivity at point p (Ω·m); C_p is the soil volumetric heat capacity at point p (J/(m³·°C)); ρ_m is the soil resistivity of the burial layer (Ω·m); θ_c is the maximum natural soil temperature (°C); θ_my is the maximum allowable temperature of the grounding electrode (°C); T_0 is the duration of operation at normal rated current (s).

variable_definitions.name: Coke cross-section side length
variable_definitions.meaning: Cross-sectional size (side length) of the coke (charcoal) material

variable_definitions.name: Coordination factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor related to soil characteristics

variable_definitions.name: Burial-layer resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Soil resistivity at the electrode burial layer

variable_definitions.name: Leakage current density
variable_definitions.meaning: Current per unit length leaking into the earth at point p

variable_definitions.name: Rated operation time
variable_definitions.meaning: Duration of operation at rated current

variable_definitions.name: Soil resistivity at point p
variable_definitions.meaning: Soil resistivity at the specified location p

variable_definitions.name: Soil volumetric heat capacity at point p
variable_definitions.meaning: Heat storage capacity of soil at the specified location p

variable_definitions.name: Maximum allowable temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum allowable operating temperature of the material/electrode

variable_definitions.name: Maximum natural soil temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum natural temperature of the surrounding soil

potential_questions: Given k=1.2, ρ_m=60Ω·m, τ_p=1.8A/m, T_0=3600s, ρ_p=55Ω·m, C_p=2.5×10^6J/(m³·°C), θ_my=90°C, θ_c=25°C, calculate the minimum coke cross-section side length S_p
task_description: 1. Compute temperature difference \(\Delta\theta=\theta_{\mathrm{my}}-\theta_c\). 2. Compute denominator \(16\rho_p C_p\Delta\theta\). 3. Compute \(T_0/(16\rho_p C_p\Delta\theta)\). 4. Take square root of the result from step 3. 5. Compute \(k\times\rho_m\times\tau_p\times\) (result of step 4). 6. Output \(S_p\ge\) the result from step 5.

full_context: Grounding electrode line conductors should preferably be arranged in a horizontally symmetrical configuration. For spans below 1000m, the horizontal conductor spacing should be calculated approximately by Eq. (10.2.2): D = 0.5 + 0.65√f + A. Where: D is the horizontal spacing between conductors (m); f is the maximum conductor sag (m); A is the icing increment constant for spacing (take 0m for 10mm icing, 0.5m for 20–30mm icing, and 1m for 40mm and above icing).

variable_definitions.name: Horizontal conductor spacing
variable_definitions.meaning: Spacing between conductors in the horizontal direction

variable_definitions.name: Maximum conductor sag
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum downward sag of the conductor under self-weight and load

variable_definitions.name: Icing increment constant
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction constant accounting for the effect of ice thickness

potential_questions: Given sag f=20m and icing thickness 25mm, calculate horizontal spacing D
task_description: 1. Obtain \(f\) and icing thickness. 2. Determine \(A\) based on icing thickness. 3. Compute \(\sqrt{f}\). 4. Compute \(0.65\sqrt{f}\). 5. Compute \(D=0.5+0.65\sqrt{f}+A\). 6. Output \(D\).

full_context: Insulation coordination of grounding electrode lines shall ensure safe and reliable operation under normal working voltage conditions. The operating line-to-ground voltage on a grounding electrode line can be calculated by Eq. (10.2.3): U_p = I_N (R_e + R_0 L_p). Where: U_p is the voltage at point p on the grounding electrode line (V); I_N is the rated DC current of the system (A); R_e is the grounding resistance of the electrode to remote earth (Ω); R_0 is the resistance per unit length of the grounding electrode line (Ω/km); L_p is the distance from point p to the grounding electrode (km).

variable_definitions.name: Voltage at point p
variable_definitions.meaning: Line-to-ground voltage at any point on the line

variable_definitions.name: Rated DC current
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated operating current of the system

variable_definitions.name: Grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Grounding resistance of the electrode to remote earth

variable_definitions.name: Unit-length resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Resistance per kilometer of the line

variable_definitions.name: Distance from grounding electrode
variable_definitions.meaning: Distance from measurement point to the grounding electrode

potential_questions: Given I_N=3000A, R_e=0.2Ω, R_0=0.15Ω/km, L_p=5km, calculate voltage U_p at point p
task_description: 1. Obtain \(I_N\), \(R_e\), \(R_0\), and \(L_p\). 2. Compute \(R_0\times L_p\). 3. Compute \(R_e+R_0L_p\). 4. Compute \(U_p=I_N\times(R_e+R_0L_p)\). 5. Output \(U_p\).

full_context: When DC current continuously flows through a grounding electrode into the earth, the soil temperature at the electrode site will rise slowly. According to thermodynamics, the soil temperature at any point near the grounding electrode at time t can be described by Eq. (A.3): θ(t) = θ_s (1 − e^(−t/T)) + θ_c. Where: θ(t) is the soil temperature at time t (°C); θ_s is the steady-state soil temperature at the grounding electrode (°C); θ_c is the ambient (t=0) temperature (°C); T is the thermal time constant of the grounding electrode (s).

variable_definitions.name: Soil temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Soil temperature at time t

variable_definitions.name: Steady-state temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Final steady-state temperature

variable_definitions.name: Ambient temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Initial soil temperature

variable_definitions.name: Time
variable_definitions.meaning: Duration of current flow

variable_definitions.name: Thermal time constant
variable_definitions.meaning: Time constant of temperature rise

potential_questions: Given θ_s=70°C, θ_c=20°C, T=1800s, calculate soil temperature θ(t) at t=900s
task_description: 1. Obtain \(\theta_s\), \(\theta_c\), \(T\), and \(t\). 2. Compute \(t/T\). 3. Compute \(e^{-t/T}\). 4. Compute \(1-e^{-t/T}\). 5. Compute \(\theta_s\times(1-e^{-t/T})\). 6. Compute \(\theta(t)=\theta_s(1-e^{-t/T})+\theta_c\). 7. Output \(\theta(t)\).

full_context: In a uniform current field, the thermal time constant of a grounding electrode can be calculated by Eq. (A.4): T = (C/(2λ)) × (R_e A/ρ)². Where: T is the thermal time constant of the grounding electrode (s); R_e is the grounding resistance of the electrode (Ω); A is the coke (charcoal) surface area (m²); ρ is the soil resistivity (Ω·m); C is the soil volumetric heat capacity (J/(m³·°C)); λ is the soil thermal conductivity (W/(m·°C)).

variable_definitions.name: Thermal time constant
variable_definitions.meaning: Time constant required for temperature to reach steady state

variable_definitions.name: Soil volumetric heat capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Heat storage capacity of the soil

variable_definitions.name: Soil thermal conductivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Soil heat conduction capability

variable_definitions.name: Grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Grounding resistance of the electrode to earth

variable_definitions.name: Coke surface area
variable_definitions.meaning: Heat dissipation surface area of the coke (charcoal) material

variable_definitions.name: Soil resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Electrical resistivity characteristic of the soil

potential_questions: Given C=2.5×10^6J/(m³·°C), λ=1.8W/(m·°C), R_e=0.25Ω, A=15m², ρ=60Ω·m, calculate thermal time constant T
task_description: 1. Compute \(R_e\times A\). 2. Compute \((R_e\times A)/\rho\). 3. Square the result from step 2. 4. Compute \(C/(2\lambda)\). 5. Compute \(T=[C/(2\lambda)]\times[(R_e A/\rho)^2]\). 6. Output \(T\).

full_context: Horizontal equivalent resistivity calculation: suppose N measurement points are taken within the detection depth of the m-th layer, giving soil resistivity values ρ_1, ρ_2, ..., ρ_N, and a grounding conductor of length L is buried in the m-th layer. The equivalent resistivity can be calculated by Eq. (A.6): ρ_m = L/Σ(ΔL_n/ρ_n). Where: ρ_m is the equivalent resistivity of the grounding electrode burial layer (Ω·m); L is the total length of the grounding conductor (m); ΔL_n is the conductor length in segment n (m); ρ_n is the soil resistivity of segment n (Ω·m).

variable_definitions.name: Equivalent resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent resistivity of layered soil

variable_definitions.name: Total grounding conductor length
variable_definitions.meaning: Total length of the grounding conductor

variable_definitions.name: Segment length
variable_definitions.meaning: Length of the grounding conductor in segment n

variable_definitions.name: Segment resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Soil resistivity in segment n

potential_questions: Given total grounding conductor length L=60m divided into 3 segments: ΔL₁=20m, ρ₁=100Ω·m; ΔL₂=25m, ρ₂=80Ω·m; ΔL₃=15m, ρ₃=120Ω·m. Calculate equivalent resistivity ρ_m.
task_description: 1. Initialize \(\text{sum}=0\). 2. For each segment, compute \(\Delta L_n/\rho_n\). 3. Accumulate all \(\Delta L_n/\rho_n\) values into \(\text{sum}\). 4. Compute \(\rho_m=L/\text{sum}\). 5. Output \(\rho_m\).

full_context: The overall equivalent resistivity of the grounding electrode site can be expressed by Eq. (A.7): ρ_d = ρ_m (R_e/R). Where: ρ_d is the overall equivalent resistivity (Ω·m); ρ_m is the equivalent resistivity of the grounding electrode burial layer; R_e is the measured grounding resistance of the electrode (Ω); R is the grounding resistance of the electrode assuming uniform and isotropic soil with resistivity ρ (Ω).

variable_definitions.name: Overall equivalent resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Overall equivalent electrical resistivity characteristic of the soil

variable_definitions.name: Burial-layer equivalent resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent soil resistivity of the electrode burial layer

variable_definitions.name: Measured grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Actual measured grounding resistance value

variable_definitions.name: Uniform-soil grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Grounding resistance assuming uniform soil

potential_questions: Given ρ_m=50Ω·m, R_e=0.15Ω, R=0.18Ω, calculate overall equivalent resistivity ρ_d
task_description: 1. Obtain \(\rho_m\), \(R_e\), and \(R\). 2. Compute \(R_e/R\). 3. Compute \(\rho_d=\rho_m\times(R_e/R)\). 4. Output \(\rho_d\).

full_context: The capacitor capacity at which harmonic resonance occurs can be calculated by Eq. (B.1): Q_cx = S_d (1/n² - K). Where: Q_cx is the capacitor capacity corresponding to n-th harmonic resonance (Mvar); S_d is the bus short-circuit capacity at the installation location of the shunt capacitor bank (MVA); n is the harmonic order, i.e., the ratio of the harmonic frequency to the grid fundamental frequency; K is the ratio of the inductive reactance of the series reactor to the capacitive reactance of the shunt capacitor bank (reactor rate).

variable_definitions.name: Resonant capacitor capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Capacitor capacity corresponding to n-th harmonic resonance

variable_definitions.name: Bus short-circuit capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Bus short-circuit capacity at the shunt capacitor installation location

variable_definitions.name: Harmonic order
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of harmonic frequency to grid fundamental frequency

variable_definitions.name: Reactor rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of series reactor inductive reactance to shunt capacitor bank capacitive reactance

potential_questions: Given S_d=1000MVA, n=5, K=0.06, find Q_cx
task_description: 1. Obtain bus short-circuit capacity \(S_d\). 2. Obtain harmonic order \(n\). 3. Obtain reactor rate \(K\). 4. Compute \(1/n^2\). 5. Compute \((1/n^2-K)\). 6. Compute \(Q_{cx}=S_d\times(1/n^2-K)\). 7. Output \(Q_{cx\,}\).

full_context: When a single capacitor bank with the same reactor rate is switched in, or when additional banks are switched in, the inrush current shall be calculated by Eq. (B.2): I_ym* = (1/√K)(1 - β Q_0/Q) + 1, where β = 1 - 1/√(1 + Ω/(K S_d)), and Q = Q' + Q_0. I_ym* is the per-unit value of the inrush current peak (with the peak rated current of the switched-in capacitor bank as base). Where: Q is the total capacitor-bank capacity installed on the same bus (Q = Q' + Q_0) (Mvar); Q_0 is the capacity of the capacitor bank being switched on (Mvar); Q' is the total capacity of all capacitor banks currently in operation (Mvar); β is the source influence factor; S_d is the bus short-circuit capacity at the shunt capacitor installation point (same as Eq. B.1) (MVA); K is the reactor rate (same as Eq. B.1); Ω is the total capacity (Ω = Q) (Mvar).

variable_definitions.name: Per-unit peak inrush current
variable_definitions.meaning: Per-unit inrush current peak referenced to the peak rated current of the switched-in capacitor bank

variable_definitions.name: Total capacitor-bank capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Total capacitor-bank capacity installed on the same bus (Q=Q'+Q_0)

variable_definitions.name: Switched-in capacitor-bank capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Capacity of the capacitor bank being switched on

variable_definitions.name: In-service capacitor-bank capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Total capacity of capacitor banks currently in operation

variable_definitions.name: Source influence factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor reflecting the influence of source/system characteristics on inrush current

variable_definitions.name: Total capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Total capacity of capacitor banks (equal to Q)

variable_definitions.name: Bus short-circuit capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Bus short-circuit capacity at the capacitor installation point (as in Eq. B.1)

variable_definitions.name: Reactor rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Series reactor inductive reactance / shunt capacitor capacitive reactance (as in Eq. B.1)

potential_questions: Given K=0.05, Q0=20Mvar, Q'=60Mvar, S_d=1000MVA, find I_ym*
task_description: 1. Compute total capacity \(Q=Q'+Q_0\). 2. Set \(\Omega=Q\). 3. Compute \(\beta=1-1/\sqrt{1+\Omega/(K S_d)}\). 4. Compute \(I_{ym}^{*}=(1/\sqrt{K})\times\left(1-\beta\,\frac{Q_0}{Q}\right)+1\). 5. Output \(I_{ym}^{*}\).

full_context: When multiple capacitor banks with two different reactor rates are switched in additionally, if Q/(K_1 S_u) ≪ 2/3, the inrush current may be calculated by Eq. (B.3): I_ym* = 1/√K_1 + 1. This is a special simplified case for inrush current calculation. Where: I_ym* is the per-unit value of the inrush current peak; K_1 is the reactor rate of the capacitor bank being switched in; Q is the total capacitor-bank capacity (Mvar); S_u is the bus short-circuit capacity (MVA), with the same meaning as S_d.

variable_definitions.name: Per-unit peak inrush current
variable_definitions.meaning: Per-unit inrush current peak referenced to the peak rated current of the switched-in capacitor bank

variable_definitions.name: Reactor rate of switched-in bank
variable_definitions.meaning: Reactor rate of the capacitor bank being switched in

variable_definitions.name: Total capacitor-bank capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Total capacitor-bank capacity on the bus (as defined in Eq. B.2)

variable_definitions.name: Bus short-circuit capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Bus short-circuit capacity (same meaning as S_d in Eq. B.1)

potential_questions: Given K1=0.05, find I_ym*
task_description: 1. Obtain \(K_1\) (reactor rate value). 2. Compute \(1/\sqrt{K_1}\). 3. Compute \(I_{ym}^{*}=1/\sqrt{K_1}+1\). 4. Output \(I_{ym}^{*}\).

full_context: The maximum number of parallel capacitor units for series-parallel capacitors shall be calculated by Eq. (B.4): M_ad ≤ (259E_zx/Ω_ed) + 1. Where: M_ad is the maximum number of capacitor units in parallel per series section per phase (or per arm for double-star connection); E_zx is the capacitor casing rupture energy guaranteed by the manufacturer (kJ); Ω_ed is the rated capacity of a single capacitor unit (kvar).

variable_definitions.name: Maximum number of parallel units
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum allowable number of parallel capacitor units per series section per phase

variable_definitions.name: Rupture energy
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum rupture energy the capacitor casing can withstand (guaranteed value)

variable_definitions.name: Rated capacity per unit
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated reactive power capacity of a single capacitor unit

potential_questions: Given E_zx=5kJ and Ω_ed=100kvar, find M_ad
task_description: 1. Obtain rupture energy \(E_{zx}\). 2. Obtain rated capacity per unit \(\Omega_{ed}\). 3. Compute \(259\times E_{zx}/\Omega_{ed}\). 4. Compute \(M_{ad}=259\times E_{zx}/\Omega_{ed}+1\). 5. Round \(M_{ad}\) to an integer. 6. Output \(M_{ad}\).
